rulings.Case Title: PHILIP B OBBARD VS. STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Philip Obbard Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate. (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) Part 3 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 2, Petitioner Philip Obbard Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate. (Part 3 of 3) To avoid a conflict with the equal protection clauses of the United States and California Constitutions that arises from the State Bar's interpretation and in fidelity with the important principle of statutory interpretation that wherever possible a statute should be interpreted to avoid constitutional infirmity, the State Bar's interpretation must be rejected and Mr. Obbard's interpretation adopted. (City of Cerritos v. State of California (2015) 239 Cal. App. 4th 1020, 1035). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Mr. Obbard is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: PHILIP B OBBARD VS. STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only. Part 2 Of 2
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 2, PETITIONER PHILIP OBBARD Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate. (part 2 of 3) The decisive factor in choosing between the two interpretations is that the State Bar's interpretation of section 6070(c) presents a serious equal protection problem because there is no rational basis why California trial court attorneys should be required to comply with the State Bar MCLE program when attorneys employed by the California Supreme Court, California Courts of Appeal and California Judicial Council are exempt. None of the State Bar's asserted "rational reasons" for this distinction - "(1) trial court employees' traditional status as county employees, (2) preservation of employee benefits following the transition of trial courts from county to state-supported funding, and (3) retention of trial courts' ability to locally set employment conditions, salaries, and benefits" (State Bar Memo 14:15-19) - are related to the objectives of the MCLE statutes. Those statutes were enacted for "consumer protection" and are designed to ensure minimum knowledge and competency of attorneys. (Warden v. State Bar (1999) 21 Cal. 4th 628, 645-47). While the equal protection rational basis test poses a low bar and few statutes are unable to meet it, it is not illusory. Per longstanding California law, as explained by Justice Roger Traynor, to satisfy the rational basis test the challenged statutory classification must "bear a reasonable relationship to the objectives that the Legislature sought to achieve by enacting" the statute. (Werner v. Southern California Associated Newspapers (1950) 35 Cal. 2d 121, 131-32). Because none of the State Bar's asserted "rational reasons" for the distinction between California trial attorneys and attorneys employed by the California Supreme Court, California Courts of Appeal and California Judicial Council have anything to do with the objectives of the MCLE program and this court has been unable to think of any "plausible reason" for the differential treatment that is related to the objectives of the MCLE program, adoption of the State Bar's interpretation of section 6070(c) as requiring California trial court attorneys to comply with the MCLE program would render that section constitutionally infirm. For instance, California trial court attorneys are not more likely than the other judicial branch attorneys to represent clients nor are they more likely to need continuing education courses. (Compare Warden (there are "plausible reasons" for exempting retired judges, state officers and law school professors from the MCLE program but not other licensed attorneys due to the former being less likely than the latter to represent clients on a full-time basis and the former being less likely to need continuing education than the latter)). (End of part 2 of 3)

rulings.Case Title: PHILIP B OBBARD VS. STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 2, PETITIONER PHILIP OBBARD Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate. (part 1 of 3) Judge Harold Kahn, who is hearing this matter, discloses that he knows, has worked with and is friends with many attorneys who are now or in the past have been employed by the San Francisco Superior Court, but believes that he is and can be fair and impartial to both sides of this case. Petitioner/plaintiff Philip Obbard's petition for writ of mandate is granted and his procedurally invalid request for relief on his complaint for declaratory relief is off calendar as moot. Mr. Obbard is entitled to the issuance of a writ directed to respondent/defendant State Bar of California mandating that the State Bar cease requiring attorneys employed by a California Superior Court to comply with the State Bar's mandatory continuing legal education program. The State Bar's interpretation of the phrase "employees of the State of California" in Business and Professions Code 6070(c) as applied to California trial court attorneys is not entitled to any deference because there is no indication that the State Bar's interpretation has been longstanding, consistent or the result of any considered analysis apart from the analysis conducted for this case. Both Mr. Obbard and the State Bar proffer reasonable, albeit wholly inconsistent, interpretations that are at least arguably supported by the history and structure of the employment status of California trial court attorneys. Neither interpretation is mandated by the text of the MCLE statutes, their legislative history, the various provisions of the California Constitution and statutes directly or indirectly addressing the employment status of trial court employees or case law construing the MCLE statutes or the provisions of the California Constitution and statutes addressing the employment status of trial court employees. While California trial court attorneys are not county employees and are properly characterized as "superior court employees," it is far from clear whether "superior court employees" is a subset of or distinct from "state employees." (end of part 1)

rulings.Case Title: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 3, RESPONDENT STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION. (part 1 of 2) Respondent/defendant State of California's demurrer to both causes of action in the petition for writ of mandate and complaint for declaratory and injunctive relief filed by petitioner/plaintiff City and County of San Francisco is sustained with ten days leave to amend to give CCSF the opportunity to name a State agency and/or officer as a respondent/defendant. Per binding case law, an action seeking an order preventing the enforcement of a State statute must seek such relief against a State agency and/or officer. For guidance of the parties going forward, once CCSF has filed an amended petition/complaint naming a State agency and/or officer as a respondent/defendant and assuming CCSF re-alleges the substantive allegations in the petition/complaint, the amended petition/complaint will contain sufficient allegations showing that the limitations on business licenses and license fees for TNCs in Business and Professions Code 16550-16550-.2 are invalid as to CCSF to warrant overruling a demurrer made on the grounds that the sections 16550-16550.2 can be validly enforced against CCSF. The applicable test to determine the validity of sections 16550-16550.2 as to a charter city such as CCSF is the "four-part test" enunciated in California Fed. Savings & Loan Assn. v. City of Los Angeles (1991) 54 Cal. 3d 1, 16-17. (State Building and Construction Trades Council of California, AFL-CIO v. City of Vista (2012) 54 Cal. 4th 547, 558-66). As to the first part, longstanding case law establishes that imposing requirements for business licenses and license fees on businesses operating within their borders implicates the "municipal affairs" of charter cities. As to the second part, it is indisputable that the limitations on business licenses and license fees for TNCs in sections 16550-16550-.2 conflict with CCSF's business registration ordinances as applied to TNCs. The third and fourth parts - whether business licenses and license fees requirements that are prohibited by sections 16550-16550-.2 are a "statewide concern" and, if so, whether those sections are "reasonably related to resolution of that concern" and "narrowly tailored to avoid unnecessary interference in local governance" - require factual development and are not amenable to resolution on a demurrer, notwithstanding that they are questions of law. (End of part 1 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondent State Of California Demurrer To Petition For Writ Of Mandate/ Prohibition/ Certification (For Tentaive Ruling Purposes Only) (Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 3, Respondent State Of California'S Demurrer To Petition For Writ Of Mandate/ Prohibition/ Certification (For Tentaive Ruling Purposes Only) (Part 2 Of 2) Liberally construing its petition/complaint, CCSF has adequately alleged ultimate facts showing that the limitations on business licenses and license fees in sections 16550-16550.2 might not pass muster under the third and fourth parts of the California Fed. test. Stated differently, based solely on the allegations in the petition/complaint and the few items that the State has requested be judicially noticed, it cannot be determined as a matter of law that the prohibited business licenses and license fees requirements are a statewide concern and sections 16550-16550-.2 are "reasonably related to resolution of that concern. Because of the importance of these issues, the necessarily sparse factual record that has been presented on this demurrer and the "great weight" that is accorded to "relevant facts established in trial court proceedings" when faced with a question whether a California statute can be applied constitutionally (per Art. XI, Sec. 5(a) of the California Constitution) to a charter city, the preferred course, as occurred in the trial court proceedings in California Fed., is to allow the parties to develop a factual record for resolution of these issues. (State Building, 54 Cal. 4th at 558; see also California Fed., 54 Cal. 3d at 18 (validity of state legislation as to a charter city entails consideration of the "fact-bound justification" of the legislation and whether the "showing before the superior court supports the Legislature's finding of a need for paramount state control over the aggregate ¿ tax burden on" the taxpayers/licensees)). Indeed, it is highly significant that the California Fed. decision, which also concerned the validity of state legislation prohibiting a charter city from imposing certain business licenses and license fees, is replete with references such as "expert testimony before the trial court confirmed ¿," "The evidence before the trial court ¿ supports ¿," and "Testimony before the trial court ¿ noted ¿" (California Fed., 54 Cal. 3d at 22-23). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CCSF is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: FUSIONSTORM VS. RENEE TENORIO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiff And Cross- Defendant Fusionstorms Notice Of Joint Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF FUSIONSTORM MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Plaintiff and cross-defendant FusionStorm's motion for summary judgment is granted. On FusionStorrm's complaint, it is entitled to judgment in its favor and against defendant and cross-complainant Renee Tenorio for a permanent injunction enjoining Ms. Tenorio from directly or indirectly using, disclosing or disseminating any non-public information that is contained in any document including an email that Ms. Tenorio prepared or received while she was employed at FusionStorm and was at any time maintained in a FusionStorm computer file. On Ms. Tenorio's sole remaining cause of action for breach of the severance agreement FusionStorm is entitled to judgment that Ms. Tenorio take nothing on that cause of action. Fairly read although not explicitly stated in its motion papers, FusionStorm has waived its requests for monetary relief in its complaint and seeks only permanent injunctive relief on its two causes of action for misappropriation of trade secrets and civil liability for violation of Penal Code 502. FusionStorm did not satisfy its summary motion burden of showing that Ms. Tenorio misappropriated any information that was the subject of reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of that information (Civil Code 3246.1(d)(2) and thus FusionStorm is not entitled to judgment in its favor on its trade secret claim. FusionStorm satisfied its summary motion burden of showing that Ms. Tenorio knowing accessed and without permission took non-public data from FusionStorm's computer system and that FusionStorm could possibly be harmed if Ms. Tenorio uses, discloses or disseminates that information. Because Ms. Tentoro did not submit any evidence creating a triable dispute on those issues, FusionStorm is entitled to injunctive relief on its section 502 claim. (Penal Code 502(c)(2) and (e)(1)). FusionStorm also satisfied its summary motion burden that Ms. Tenorio remains in possession of FusionStorm proprietary information and thus, per the first paragraph on page 4 of Ms. Tenorio's severance agreement, a condition precedent to FusionStorm's performance under that agreement has not occurred and FusionStorm is not liable to Ms. Tenorio for any non-performance by it under that agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for FusionStorm is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: THELMA BRUNN VS. CATHAY MORTUARY - (WAH SANG) ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Deposition And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF THELMA BRUNN's Motion To Compel Deposition And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Thelma Brunn's Motion to Compel Deposition and for Sanctions is GRANTED. Defendant Cathay Mortuary-(Wah Sang) is ordered to produce its person most knowledgeable (pursuant to the categories stated in plaintiff's January 29, 2018 Amended Depo Notice for PMK at Cathan Mortuary) on June 5, 2018 at 1:30 at the Law Offices of Bruce S. Osterman, 2300 Contra Costa Blvd., Suite 320, Pleasant Hill, CA. Sanctions in the amount of $1500 are awarded against Defendant Cathay Mortuary - (Way Sang) in favor of Plaintiff Brunn. Sanctions are payable on or before June 5, 2018. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ELLYN WEISS VS. JULIE ALMEIDA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Julie Almeida, Robert Almeida Demurrer To Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) (Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 12, Defendants Julie Almeida, Robert Almeida's Demurrer To Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) (Part 2 Of 2) While the court can take judicial notice that an emergency protective order was issued against Ms. Weiss, the court cannot take judicial notice of the truth of the statements therein. Further, an EPO is often issued based on one side's version of events and has no preclusive effect in any subsequent litigation and cannot be used to show any misconduct by Ms. Weiss on this demurrer. This is particularly true here where the EPO sought to be judicially noticed has no entries in the application portion and thus does not even purport to provide any specific facts for the issuance of the EPO, which probably makes the EPO invalid as a matter of law. Nor does the joint stipulation regarding the division of property have any bearing on this demurrer since, even if its terms were judicially noticeable, nothing in those terms show that any claim asserted by Ms. Weiss in her complaint is in any way deficient or otherwise demurrable. The facts alleged by Ms. Weiss, if true -- and the court is required to liberally construe them and assume their truth on this demurrer -- sufficiently show that the Almeidas intruded into and trespassed on Ms. Weiss' private space in her father's home, committed extreme and outrageous conduct toward Ms. Weiss, and violated a duty they owed to Ms. Weiss founded in Civil Code 1714(a). Defendants' argument that Ms. Weiss has not properly pled a common law fraud claim is curious given that Ms. Weiss has not purported to allege such a claim, although she does allege that conduct that arguably could sound in fraud is evidence for the non-common law fraud claims she has alleged. Because the demurrer is overruled as to all causes of action, the court chooses not to address the Almeidas' request for fees and costs per CCP 128.5 and damages for malicious prosecution except to say that there has been no showing that the complaint runs afoul of section 128.5 and damages for malicious prosecution cannot be awarded on a demurrer. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Weiss is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: ELLYN WEISS VS. JULIE ALMEIDA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANTS JULIE ALMEIDA, ROBERT ALMEIDA'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. (part 1 of 2) Defendants Julie and Robert Almeida's demurrer to all six causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Ellyn Weiss is overruled as to all six causes of action. Even were the court to overlook that the Almeidas' demurrer recites "facts" nowhere mentioned in the complaint or in the documents sought to be judicially noticed (such as decedent's estrangement from Ms. Weiss), all of the many arguments made by the Almeidas lack merit. The argument that this case should have been filed in Santa Clara Probate Court is not appropriate on a demurrer and needs to be made on a venue motion, but is also substantively wrong since it is based on the erroneous premise that this case seeks to contest decedent's trust, which Ms. Weiss disclaims doing. Ms. Weiss has sufficiently alleged her standing to bring the first cause of action for financial elder abuse per Estate of Lowrie (2004) 118 Cal. App. 4th 220. The no contest clause in the trust documents is immaterial since Ms. Weiss is not contesting the trust and, even if she was, the no contest clause would not bar her from alleging a claim to do so, but would only penalize her if she were unsuccessful on that claim. The argument that the first cause of action is barred by the 120 day limitations period fails since Ms. Weiss is not contesting the trust and, even if she was, there is no document showing when the Probate Code 16061.7 notice was served on Ms. Weiss and, even if there was such a document, the court could not take judicial notice of it since the date of service is not a subject beyond reasonable dispute. None of the cases cited by defendants for the proposition that Ms. Weiss should be deemed to be contesting the trust concern a claim for financial elder abuse and are readily distinguishable from this case. Moreover, the language and logic, if not the holding of Lowrie makes clear that a financial elder abuse claim is separate and distinct from a trust contest and is not governed by the rules regarding trust contests. Ms. Weiss has adequately alleged her claims for defamation, invasion of privacy, trespass and intentional and negligent infliction of emotional distress. The face of the complaint does not disclose that any of the alleged defamatory statements are true nor does it show that those statements (particularly the alleged statement that Ms. Weiss had been stealing from decedent) are necessarily barred by the litigation privilege. (End of part 1 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH FOX VS. NORMA R. TORRES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Subpoenas For Production Of Consumer And Employment Records Or In The Alternative For Order Modifying The Subpoenas And Issueing A Protective Order And Point And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 23, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF JOSEPH FOX's Motion To Quash Subpoenas For Production Of Consumer And Employment Records Or In The Alternative For Order Modifying The Subpoenas And Issueing A Protective Order And Point And Authorities In Support Thereof. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)
